title: Food Deserts Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.
plain_summary: The Food Deserts Act creates a new government program to help bring grocery stores to communities that don't have good access to healthy food. The Department of Agriculture will give money to states, and then states will use that money to make low-cost loans to grocery stores. To get these loans, stores must sell healthy foods like fresh fruits and vegetables at affordable prices and hire workers from the local community. The program gets 150 million dollars to start in 2026 and focuses on helping areas with high poverty and food insecurity.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.
subjects: Urban and suburban affairs and development

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Food Deserts Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Grant Program to Establish Grocery Stores in Underserved Communities
sections.text: The Secretary of Agriculture will create a program that gives money to states. States will use this money to set up revolving funds (money that gets loaned out and paid back to be used again). These funds will help start and run grocery stores in underserved communities (areas where people have trouble getting food). States must run these funds through a state agency that has the right powers to manage the money according to this law. The revolving fund money can only be used to make loans for four things. First, to open a grocery store in an underserved community, but not to build a new building from scratch. Second, to help an existing grocery store in an underserved community keep operating. Third, to help people get access to healthy food. Fourth, to help a business that got a loan under this program if that business is in a community that would be underserved without the business being there. States can only give loans to businesses that meet certain requirements. The business must be a grocery store or plan to become one after opening. It must focus on unprocessed healthy foods. It must sell a variety of fresh fruits and vegetables. It must sell staple foods (basic foods people need regularly). It must have a plan to keep these foods in stock as much as possible. It must charge affordable prices at or below what other stores charge. The business must either know how to run a grocery store or have partnerships with groups that can teach them how to run food businesses. The business must provide at least twenty percent of the loan amount from their own non-federal money. States should give priority to businesses that meet additional criteria. These include hiring workers who live in the underserved community the store will serve. They should offer classes or information about healthy eating. They should buy food from local urban farms and gardens when possible. They should already have relationships with grocery supply companies. Any business that wants a loan must apply to the state. The state decides what information to require and when applications are due. Loans from the revolving fund can only be used for the four purposes listed earlier. States must charge interest rates at or below what banks normally charge. States can make loans with no interest at all. Loans cannot last longer than thirty years or longer than the expected useful life of the project, whichever is shorter. States can give the loan money all at once or spread it out over several years if needed. A state cannot give any one business more than ten percent of all the money available for loans in any single year. Businesses must start paying back the loan no later than one year after they get the money. All loans must be completely paid back by the end of the loan term. Businesses must set up a specific source of money to pay back their loans. When businesses pay back their loans with interest, that money goes back into the revolving fund to be loaned out again. States can charge businesses an administrative fee of up to four percent of the loan amount. States use these fees to manage the revolving fund and run the program. The Secretary of Agriculture will provide technical help to businesses that get loans. This help includes advice about getting food, storing food, and other business operations. If a business that got a loan goes bankrupt, the loan debt gets paid before other debts. If a community was underserved when loans were made but later becomes well-served with food options, businesses in that community cannot get new loans under this program. However, their existing loan agreements stay the same. If a nonprofit organization or city-owned business gets a loan and makes money from it, that money must be put back into the business or used to keep the business running.

sections.title: Section 3 Capitalization Grants to Fund State Revolving Funds
sections.text: To be eligible for a capitalization grant (the money the federal government gives to states to start their revolving funds), a state must do two things. First, it must set up a revolving fund that follows all the rules in this law. Second, it must create a process for businesses to apply for loans and set standards for deciding which businesses get loans, following the requirements in Section 2. When a state gets a capitalization grant, it must put all that money into its revolving fund. Each year, the Secretary of Agriculture will divide the available money among eligible states based on population. Each state gets a share equal to the number of people living in underserved communities in that state compared to the total number of people living in underserved communities in all eligible states.

sections.title: Section 4 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress authorizes spending one hundred fifty million dollars to carry out this law in fiscal year two thousand twenty-six.

sections.title: Section 5 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms have specific meanings. A capitalization grant means money given to a state under this program. Healthful food means food that follows the most recent Dietary Guidelines for Americans (official government advice about healthy eating). A grocery store means a retail store that makes most of its money selling food that people buy to prepare and eat at home. The program means the program described in Section 2. A program participant means a business that has received a loan under this program. A revolving fund means money set aside by a state to hold and manage the capitalization grant money. The Secretary means the Secretary of Agriculture. Staple food has the same meaning as defined in section two hundred forty-three of the Department of Agriculture Reorganization Act of nineteen ninety-four. A state includes the fifty states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. An underserved community has the same meaning as defined in section three hundred ten B of the Consolidated Farm and Rural Development Act.
